title: practical english usage - Part 64
content:

Can is not normally used to talk about the chance that something will happen or grammar •  72  may and might: some special uses May she rest in peace. (prayer for a dead person) May the New Year bring you all your heart desires.

May you both be very happy! May God be with you.

May often comes at the beginning of the sentence.

Let us pray that peace may soon return to our troubled land.

I hope that the young couple may enjoy many years of happiness together.

May (but not might) is used in formal expressions of wishes and hopes.

1 may     in  wishes     and   hopes 72 may     and   mi  ght:   some    special     uses   ________ Let's ask Amy. She might know which is the right one. It's worth a try.

- I've got all John's keys here. One of them ought to open the safe.

Dan may be in the office now - I don't know what time he starts work.

- Anna should be in the office now - it's ten o'clock.

that we  have a good reason for thinking it is true or will happen. Compare: May, might and could simply say that something is possible, without suggesting they suggest that we  have a good reason for this belief.

We use should and ought to to express a deduction that something is probable: 10 the   difference        between       may/might/could                and   should/ought             to For might/could have ... used to criticise people for not doing things, ►    80.3.

(More normal:...  You might/could have killed yourself.) You were stupid to try climbing up there. You may have killed yourself.

that this is not correct.

May have .. .is now sometimes used with this meaning too; some people feel If she hadn't been so bad-tempered, I might/could have married her.

yourself.

You were stupid to try climbing up there. You might/could have killed have...

To  say that something was possible but did not happen, we  can use might/could 9 might/could            have      ...: You     might/could            have      killed      yourself.

By the end of this year I might have saved some money.

I'll try phoning him,  but he may have gone out by now.

May/might have . .. can sometimes refer to the present or future.

'What was that noise?' 'It might/could have been a cat.’ missed ...) 'Polly's very late.' 'She may have missed her train.’ (= It is possible that she past, we  can use may/might/could have + past participle.

However, to say that it is possible that something happened or was true in the 8  may/might/could                have     .. .: She     may     have      missed        her    train.

Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar •  73   strong obligation: must, will Why must  you always bang the door?

Must I clean all the rooms?

In questions, we  use must to ask about what the hearer thinks is necessary.

2 questions:      Must     I. . .?

You really must  come and see us soon.

Must is common in emphatic invitations.

You must  be here before eight o'clock tomorrow.

I really must  stop smoking.

British industry must  improve its productivity.

Plants must  get enough light and water if they are to grow well.

strong advice and orders to ourselves or other people.

In affirmative statements, we  can use must to say what is necessary, and to give 1  statements:       I really      must      stop     smoking.

American English.

influence. wouldn’ t be any different if we  were at home.) This trip isn't much  fun. We might just as well be back home. (= Things Might as well is also used to compare one unpleasant situation with another.

We'd better eat. (= We   ought to eat; there is a good reason to eat.) We may as well eat. (= There is nothing more interesting to do.) Note the difference between may/might as well and had better (►   77). Compare: 'Shall we go and see Fred?' 'OK, might as well.' There's nobody interesting to talk to. We may as well go home.
